tasks.idea:
Update the LaunchPilot dashboard to add a “Connected Public Repos” section using the whitelist data.

The section should show only public repositories that are connected to LaunchPilot/S.A.G.E. by reading them from the whitelist fields published in tasks.json.

Requirements:
- Add a new section to index.html called “Connected Public Repos”.
- The repos must come from whitelist data, not inferred from recent tasks.
- Support these whitelist field names from tasks.json:
  - whitelistedRepos
  - whitelisted_repos
  - publicWhitelist
  - public_whitelist
  - allowedRepos
  - allowed_repos
  - repos
- If a whitelisted repo is marked private, skip it completely.
- Support privacy fields like:
  - private: true
  - isPrivate: true
  - visibility: "private"
  - public: false
- Only render repos that are public or not explicitly marked private.
- Show each repo in a clean card or table with:
  - repository name
  - agent, if provided: LaunchPilot, S.A.G.E., or Both
  - allowed work / purpose, if provided
  - optional GitHub or website link, if provided
- Add a short note saying private repositories may exist in the whitelist but are filtered out before display.
- Add static fallback examples using only public repos.
- Match the existing dashboard design using the current cards, chips, tables, spacing, and responsive layout.
- Keep the dashboard read-only.
- Do not add React, build tools, dependencies, or secrets.

tasks.idea:
Fix my GitHub Pages deployment workflow for my React + Vite app.
Project:
Routine No. 1
Repo:
routine-number-one
Problem:
GitHub Actions builds successfully, but deployment fails with:
“Missing environment. Ensure your workflow’s deployment job has an environment.”
Goal:
When I push to the main branch, the app should automatically build and deploy to GitHub Pages.
Requirements:
Create or fix:
.github/workflows/deploy.yml
Workflow should trigger on:
on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main
Use official GitHub Pages GitHub Actions:
actions/checkout
actions/setup-node
actions/configure-pages
actions/upload-pages-artifact
actions/deploy-pages
The workflow must:
checkout repo
setup Node 20
run npm ci
run npm run build
upload dist folder
deploy to GitHub Pages
IMPORTANT:
The deployment job MUST include the required environment configuration to fix the error:
environment:
  name: github-pages
  url: ${{ steps.deployment.outputs.page_url }}
Create a separate deploy job that:
depends on build (needs: build)
runs actions/deploy-pages@v4
Ensure GitHub Pages settings are compatible:
Repository Settings → Pages → Source = GitHub Actions
Verify Vite config uses:
base: '/routine-number-one/'
Ensure no manual deployment is required:
After setup, this should work automatically:
git push origin main
Expected result:
Every push to main automatically deploys the latest build to:
https://jodyswartz.github.io/routine-number-one/
Deliverables:
Fixed .github/workflows/deploy.yml
Explanation of what caused the deployment failure
Confirmation that pushing to main auto deploys
Any GitHub settings that must be changed

tasks.idea:
Set up GitHub Actions deployment for my React + Vite app.

Project:
Routine No. 1

Repo:
routine-number-one

Goal:
When I push to the main branch, GitHub Actions should automatically build and deploy the app to GitHub Pages.

Requirements:
1. Create a GitHub Actions workflow file:
   .github/workflows/deploy.yml

2. The workflow should run on:
   push to main

3. It should:
   - Check out the repo
   - Set up Node.js
   - Run npm ci
   - Run npm run build
   - Deploy the dist folder to GitHub Pages

4. Use official GitHub Pages actions:
   - actions/checkout
   - actions/setup-node
   - actions/configure-pages
   - actions/upload-pages-artifact
   - actions/deploy-pages

5. Ensure vite.config.js uses:
   base: '/routine-number-one/'

6. Ensure GitHub Pages settings should be:
   Source: GitHub Actions

7. Remove the need to run npm run deploy manually.

8. Keep package.json deploy scripts optional, but the GitHub Action should be the main deployment method.

Create this file:

.github/workflows/deploy.yml

Expected workflow:

name: Deploy to GitHub Pages

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main

permissions:
  contents: read
  pages: write
  id-token: write

concurrency:
  group: pages
  cancel-in-progress: false

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Setup Node
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          cache: npm

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: Build
        run: npm run build

      - name: Setup Pages
        uses: actions/configure-pages@v5

      - name: Upload artifact
        uses: actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3
        with:
          path: ./dist

      - name: Deploy to GitHub Pages
        uses: actions/deploy-pages@v4

Deliverables:
- Add the workflow file
- Explain how to enable GitHub Pages with Source: GitHub Actions
- Confirm that pushing to main deploys automatically

tasks.idea:
Fix my GitHub Pages deployment for my React + Vite app.

Project name:
Routine No. 1

Repo name:
routine-number-one

Live URL:
https://jodyswartz.github.io/routine-number-one/

Problem:
The GitHub Pages site opens but shows a blank white screen.

Please debug and fix the project.

Check and fix:
1. vite.config.js must use:
   base: '/routine-number-one/'

2. GitHub Pages must publish from:
   Branch: gh-pages
   Folder: /root

3. package.json must include:
   "predeploy": "npm run build",
   "deploy": "gh-pages -d dist"

4. gh-pages must be installed as a dev dependency.

5. The deployed gh-pages branch must contain:
   index.html
   assets/

6. Check the browser console for JavaScript runtime errors causing the blank screen.

7. Verify all imports are correct.

8. If React Router is used, replace BrowserRouter with HashRouter.

9. Make sure the app runs locally with:
   npm run dev

10. Make sure production build works with:
   npm run build

Deliverables:
- Identify the exact cause of the blank screen
- Fix the code/config
- Commit the changes
- Redeploy to GitHub Pages
- Confirm the live URL works

import { defineConfig } from 'vite'

export default defineConfig({

tasks.idea:
Build a minimalist web app called “Routine No. 1”.

Concept:
A beautifully minimal push-up set counter inspired by vintage flip clocks and Wes Anderson aesthetics. The experience should feel calm, tactile, symmetrical, and intentionally simple.

Goal:
A single-screen micro app for counting push-up sets.

Tech requirements:
- React + Vite
- Deployable to GitHub Pages
- No backend
- No database
- Local-only state using localStorage
- Mobile-first responsive design
- Clean component structure
- Production-ready code

Core functionality:
- Large centered counter in flip-clock style
- Plus (+) button increments by 1
- Minus (–) button decrements by 1
- Counter can never go below zero
- Reset button resets counter to zero
- Persist count in localStorage so refresh keeps value
- Smooth flip animation when incrementing/decrementing
- Touch-friendly controls for mobile

Design requirements:
- Wes Anderson-inspired color palette
- Symmetrical layout
- Minimalist design
- Vintage mechanical flip clock feel
- Soft shadows and subtle depth
- Calm, premium feeling
- Avoid aggressive fitness aesthetics
- Large typography for counter
- Elegant serif headings with clean sans-serif UI text
- Generous spacing
- Rounded buttons

Branding:
App name: “Routine No. 1”

Header:
ROUTINE
No. 1

Optional subtitle:
“A counter for daily repetitions.”

Color direction:
Warm cream background
Muted dusty pink
Mustard accent
Deep burgundy details
Soft shadows

UX details:
- Smooth 200–300ms flip transition
- Subtle button press feedback
- Haptic-friendly interactions (if mobile/PWA later)
- Reset should feel intentional and not destructive

Project structure:
- Clean reusable components
- Separate FlipCounter component
- Reusable Button component
- CSS modules or clean styling architecture
- Simple and maintainable

Deployment:
Include GitHub Pages deployment setup:
- Correct Vite base config
- gh-pages package
- package.json deploy scripts

Deliverables:
1. Complete project structure
2. Production-ready code
3. README.md
4. GitHub Pages deployment instructions
5. Beautiful polished UI, not generic AI styling

tasks.idea: Make a small README change for cancel testing

Improve the README by adding a “Telegram commands” section. Include a markdown table with each LaunchPilot command, what it does, and an example. Cover: /new <idea>, /repo <taskId> <owner/repo>, /run <taskId>, /status <taskId>, /logs <taskId>, /tasks, /approve <taskId>, /reject <taskId>, and /merge <taskId>. Also add a short “Typical workflow” example showing the commands in order from idea to merged deployment.

Improve the README with a beginner-friendly explanation of LaunchPilot. Add sections called “What is LaunchPilot?”, “How it works”, “Example workflow”, and “Deployment links”. The “How it works” section should describe the five-step flow: Telegram idea → Codex build → GitHub PR → Approve and merge → Open website. Include example commands using /new, /repo, /run, /status, and explain that LaunchPilot can show the live website link from Vercel or GitHub Pages after merge.

Add an animated “How it works” section to the existing index.html page. Show the LaunchPilot workflow as five steps: 1) Telegram idea, 2) Codex build, 3) GitHub PR, 4) Approve and merge, 5) Open website. Use a clean modern design with connected cards or steps, simple icons, and subtle animations such as fade-in, pulse, or step-by-step highlighting. Make it responsive, with a horizontal flow on desktop and a vertical stacked layout on mobile. Keep the existing hero section and overall style.

Improve the README with a clear overview of LaunchPilot. Explain that it is a Telegram-controlled AI coding workflow that turns ideas into GitHub pull requests, supports approve-and-merge, and can return a live website link from Vercel or GitHub Pages. Add a “How it works” section, a “Example workflow” section, and a short “Requirements” section.

Add a short one-sentence explanation for each step, keep the design clean and modern, and preserve the existing hero section.
